title: Saving NASA’s Workforce Act
plain_summary: This bill protects NASA workers from being laid off or fired until Congress approves NASA's complete budget for 2026. NASA can still fire employees if they do something seriously wrong or are not doing their job well. The law is meant to keep NASA workers safe from losing their jobs while Congress decides how much money NASA will get. It only stops NASA from firing workers for budget reasons, not for breaking rules or poor performance.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving NASA's Workforce Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Reduction in Force Moratorium at National Aeronautics and Space Administration
sections.text: Until Congress passes and makes into law the full budget for NASA for fiscal year twenty twenty six, NASA cannot lay off workers or fire employees. NASA cannot start or carry out any reduction in force (a plan to cut the number of workers). NASA also cannot force any employee to leave their job involuntarily. This rule applies to employees in the competitive service (jobs that people get through testing and competition), career employees in the excepted service (certain government jobs that do not require competitive testing), and career appointees in the Senior Executive Service (high level career government managers). NASA can still fire employees for cause, which means for serious reasons like bad behavior, not doing their job, or poor performance. The terms competitive service, excepted service, and career appointee have the same meanings as defined in sections twenty one oh two, twenty one oh three, and thirty one thirty two of title five of the United States Code. This rule works along with any other laws about taking negative action against government employees, including chapter seventy five of title five of the United States Code.
